MOLECUL AR BIOLOGY

Genome wide nucleosome landscape shapes 3D chromatin organization Shah Fouziya1†, Nils Krietenstein2†, Ulfat Syed Mir3†, Jakub Mieczkowski4, Masood A. Khan1, Aemon Baba1, Mohmmad Abaas Dar1, Mohammad Altaf3, Ajazul H. Wani1*

INTRODUCTION

Eukaryotic genomes are organized in a nonrandom hierarchical manner, and this organization is linked to the regulation of genome function. Alterations in chromatin contacts lead to altered gene expression resulting in developmental defects and diseases (1, 2). A conserved feature of chromatin organization across different species, from yeast to humans, is its hierarchical nature scaling from nucleosomes and folded domains to chromosomes (3–5). This suggests that there exist some common determinants that regulate the basic organization of genome in all eukaryotes. Studies of the threedimensional (3D) chromatin organization in different model organisms have provided a wealth of information about the molecular mechanisms involved in regulating chromatin contacts at multiple genomic and spatial scales (3–6). The organization of chromatin in yeast scales from nucleosomes to gene level folding, formation of multigene folded chromatin domains, and organization of chromosomes into a Rabl conformation, in which different chromosomes are held together by their centromeres (Fig. 1A) (5, 7). At the primary level, 147 bp of DNA are wrapped around an octamer of histone proteins forming a nucleosome. Nucleosomes are regularly positioned across the genome which results in proper spacing between them. However, at some sites like near transcription start sites (TSSs), DNA is not wrapped around a histone octamer most of the time, resulting in lower occupancy of nucleosomes at these regions as compared to flanking regions. A final primary structure property is the accessibility of DNA wrapped around histones, which differs at different nucleosomes. Application of chromosome conformation capture, imaging, as well as modeling-based studies (5, 8–11) has shown that chromatin 1

in yeast is organized into folded chromatin domains formed by preferential interactions between nucleosomes present within a few consecutive kilo–base pairs of DNA. Chromatin domains are separated by regions having fewer chromatin contacts, called domain boundaries (Fig. 1A) (5). These folded domains also seem to act as functional units in terms of the expression state of genes harbored by them or in the timing of their replication (5, 9). Various chromatin-associated factors like forkhead transcription factor (Fkh1/2), cohesin, suppressor of SUa7, gene 2 (Ssu72), and histone acetyltransferase regulator of Ty1 transposition (Rtt109) have been shown to regulate 3D chromatin organization in budding yeast (5, 8, 9). Several posttranslational histone modifications like H3K4me3 and H3K18ac are selectively enriched at boundaries. In addition, boundaries were found to have high transcription rates and nucleosome-depleted regions (NDRs) (5). Adenosine triphosphate (ATP)–dependent chromatin remodeling factors regulate the assembly, disassembly, positioning, occupancy, and spacing of nucleosomes across the genome (12–15). Depleting chromatin remodeling factors also decrease chromatin contacts (16), but their role in chromatin domain formation has not been assessed. The intrinsic ability of nucleosomal arrays to fold and the effect of changing the linker length have also been investigated in vitro and using computational modeling. In vitro salt-induced folding of oligonucleosomal arrays showed a periodic dependence on the linker length (17, 18). Polymer modeling studies predict the dependence of looping probability between different nucleosomes and chromatin folding on the linker length (18, 19). Simulations of chromatin fiber folding with heterogeneous linker lengths like those observed across genomes showed the formation of folded chromatin domains like those detected experimentally (20, 21). These studies predict that the positioning of nucleosomes along the DNA may be a fundamental principle underlying the features of chromatin domains and the boundaries separating them. However, these predictions have not been tested experimentally in vivo. Here, we investigated the relationship between nucleosome positioning, occupancy and accessibility, and 3D chromatin organization in budding yeast. These features of chromatin organization are

The hierarchical chromatin organization begins with formation of nucleosomes, which fold into chromatin domains punctuated by boundaries and ultimately chromosomes. In a hierarchal organization, lower levels shape higher levels. However, the dependence of higher-order 3D chromatin organization on the nucleosome-level organization has not been studied in cells. We investigated the relationship between nucleosome-level organization and higher-order chromatin organization by perturbing nucleosomes across the genome by deleting Imitation SWItch (ISWI) and Chromodomain Helicase DNA-binding (CHD1) chromatin remodeling factors in budding yeast. We find that changes in nucleosome-level properties are accompanied by changes in 3D chromatin organization. Shortrange chromatin contacts up to a few kilo–base pairs decrease, chromatin domains weaken, and boundary strength decreases. Boundary strength scales with accessibility and moderately with width of nucleosomedepleted region. Change in nucleosome positioning seems to alter the stiffness of chromatin, which can affect formation of chromatin contacts. Our results suggest a biomechanical “bottom-up” mechanism by which nucleosome distribution across genome shapes 3D chromatin organization.

To alter these nucleosome properties and monitor the effects on architectural features of chromatin organization, we used a triple knockout (TKO) strain of imitation switch 1 (isw1Δ), imitation switch 2 (isw2Δ), and chromodomain helicase DNA-binding (chd1Δ) adenosine triphosphatases (ATPases) and conducted a series of sequencing-based experiments. We find that the alteration of nucleosome distribution across the genome is accompanied by a change in 3D chromatin organization at multiple

scales. Our results suggest that nucleosome organization regulates higher-order chromatin organization in cells. RESULTS

Changes in the nucleosome profile are associated with changes in 3D chromatin organization To find whether a change in nucleosome landscape across the genome is accompanied by a change in the 3D organization of chromatin, we used two different chromosome conformation capture procedures depending on two different enzymes for the digestion of DNA, high-throughput chromosome conformation capture (Hi-C), and Micro-C XL. Both methods detect centromere-centromere interactions in the WT and TKO strains, visible as dots in interaction matrices (Fig. 2, A to C). We generated trans-chromosomal interaction pile-ups between all combinations of mapped centromere positions and observed a decrease in centromere-centromere interactions in the TKO strain (fig. S4, A and B). This confirms the Rabl-like chromosome conformation that is typical in yeast. At the chromosome level, we observe a decrease in contacts along the diagonal of the contact map, for example, for chromosome III (chrIII) (Fig. 2D). We compared the interaction probability curves from Micro-C XL experiments of WT and TKO strains and observed a global loss of short-range interactions (500 to 5000 bp) across all chromosomes (Fig. 2E) and an increase in long-range contacts. Similarly, a decrease in chromatin contacts is observed in TKO from Hi-C analysis (Fig. 2F). Hence, with both these distinct chromosome conformation capture experiments, we observe similar changes in global chromatin organization in TKO strain. Leveraging on the high resolution of Micro-C XL, we next analyzed the chromatin organization at the level of domains and boundaries. In 3D interaction heatmaps, we observed folded chromatin domains in the WT strain as reported before (5); however, in the case of the TKO strain, chromatin domains are not as distinct compared to WT domains (Fig. 3A). Although at a lower resolution, Hi- C contact maps also show weaker domains in TKO than WT strain (fig. S4C). We called boundary sites at 100-bp resolution using a sliding diamond of 5 kilo–base pair (kbp) using Micro-C XL data. We generated on-diagonal pile-ups of WT boundaries (insulation sites) stratified by their strength for WT and TKO 3D data (Fig. 3B). We observe that the boundary strength is weakened in the TKO condition, and interactions across the boundary sites are gained at high confidence sites (≥0.6). At the same time, we observe that short-range interactions, seen along the diagonal in regions flanking the boundaries, are decreased (Fig. 3B, bottom row). This agrees with the global decrease of short-range interactions observed in a 0.5-to 5-kbp range (Fig. 2E). Next, we compared the properties of boundary sites identified in WT and TKO strains to evaluate the relationship between chromatin organization and boundary sites. We considered boundary sites with intervals of boundary strength ranging from 0.2 to 0.4, 0.4 to 0.6, 0.6 to 0.8, and 0.8 to maximum. We consistently identified (~1.75×) more boundary sites in the TKO strain than in the WT strain (Fig. 3C). Consequently, the average distance between consecutive boundary sites in TKO is shorter than in WT (Fig. 3D). However, we observed that the insulation score at boundary sites in TKO strain is higher than in the corresponding threshold class from WT. Higher insulation scores indicate a lower strength of boundary sites. Hence, boundary sites in the TKO strain are comparably weaker than those in the WT strain (Fig. 3E). Thus, we conclude that chromatin topology is coupled to nucleosome

positioning but retains cell cycle profile and growth similar to that of wild type (WT) (fig. S1C) (12, 13, 22). The expression level of the majority of genes is not altered in this strain (see below) (12). These features of TKO strain make it an ideal system to address the relationship between nucleosome-level chromatin features and higher-order 3D organization. We used micrococcal nuclease (MNase)–titration-seq to analyze nucleosome positioning, occupancy, and accessibility in the TKO versus WT strains. MNase cuts DNA preferentially at linker regions, giving rise to fragments corresponding to the DNA that is wrapped around histone octamers. Given the differential sensitivity of different nucleosomes to MNase digestion, we used a titration of MNase coupled to next-generation sequencing to get the integrated nucleosome occupancy profile (23, 24). In agreement with previous studies (12, 13), we observed an NDR near TSSs, well-positioned +1 nucleosome, and a phased array of subsequent nucleosomes in WT strain at all MNase concentrations (Fig. 1B and fig. S2). In the TKO strain, an NDR near the TSS and a well-positioned +1 nucleosome distinguishable from +2 nucleosome are apparent at intermediate MNase concentrations, but no phasing of downstream nucleosomes is observed (Fig. 1B and figs. S2 and S3A). We also see a change in nucleosome phasing upstream of TSSs, with WT but not TKO strain having wellpositioned nucleosomes at lower MNase concentrations (Fig. 1B and fig. S2). Thus, nucleosome positioning is markedly altered in the TKO strain. MNase-titration-seq also yields information about nucleosome accessibility via a parameter called MNase accessibility (MACC) score, which gives an estimate of DNA accessibility at different genomic or organizational features (23, 24). MACC is the slope of the plot of released (nucleosomal) reads versus the log of MNase concentration; a positive slope (positive MACC) indicates high accessibility, and a negative slope (negative MACC) indicates low accessibility (24). MACC scores were computed in 200-bp genomic bins. Comparison of MACC scores between the WT and TKO strains identifies 1199 sites that are more accessible in the WT than the TKO strain and 479 sites which are more accessible in the TKO strain than the WT strain (Fig. 1C). Mapping these sites onto genomic features indicates that about 75% of more accessible sites in WT strain are present at TSSs. In the TKO strain, only 20% of more accessible sites are present at TSSs, while 60% are in gene bodies and around the TTS (fig. S3B). In the WT strain, more reads corresponding to +1 nucleosomes are released at lower MNase concentrations, and these released reads decrease with an increase in MNase concentration. But the opposite is the case with +2 and subsequent nucleosomes along the gene body (Fig. 1, B and D, and fig. S3A) (25). This implies that, for +1 nucleosomes, MACC score is positive and negative for downstream nucleosomes. However, this is not the case for +1 nucleosomes in the TKO strain. For both +1 nucleosomes and downstream nucleosomes, released reads increased with an increase in MNase concentration. This suggests that although +1 nucleosomes are well-positioned in WT as well as TKO strain, but their properties (here sensitivity to MNase) are not the same (Fig. 1D and fig. S3A). Overall, a negative correlation is observed between the change in occupancy and the change in accessibility, suggesting that a decrease in occupancy is accompanied by an increase in accessibility (Fig. 1E). More accessible sites in TKO than WT strain have lower occupancy, while less accessible sites in TKO strain (more accessible in WT) have higher occupancy in TKO strain (fig. S3, C and D). Together,

Nucleosome occupancy and accessibility correlate with domain boundary strength Most boundaries correspond to NDRs, although not all NDRs form boundaries (5). Figure 4A (top) shows a representative genomic region at which chromatin domains and their boundaries are diffused in the TKO strain. A genome browser view of the MNase sequencing (MNase-seq) data at a corresponding region (Fig. 4A, bottom) shows that the positioning of nucleosomes is altered in TKO strain around these boundaries as well as within the domain. To assess the relationship between nucleosome depletion and boundary strength, we clustered all boundaries with a boundary score higher than 0.1 into five equally sized groups stratified by boundary strength, Q1 (low) to Q5 (high) (Fig. 4B), and plotted average MNase-seq reads at these sites. Overall, all quintiles showed similar strong nucleosome depletion; however, Q3 and Q4 have an extended NDR (Fig. 4C). Consistent with this, we found a moderate increase in the width of NDRs with an increase in boundary strength but not a clear correlation with the depth of NDRs (Fig. 4C and fig. S5C). nucleosome depletion is a common feature of boundary sites, and the width of NDRs correlates with boundary strength. Given that domain boundaries have fewer contacts than flanking regions, the underlying chromatin might be more accessible than neighboring regions. To investigate this hypothesis, we computed the MACC around domain boundaries of different strengths (Q1 to Q5) (Fig. 4D). From MACC scores computed at these different classes of boundaries both in WT and TKO strain, it is evident that the boundary strength directly scales with the MACC score, with Q5 having the highest MACC and Q1 having the lowest MACC (Fig. 4D). Intersection of MACC and insulation sites revealed that about 45% of insulation sites from either of the strains fall in top 5% of MACC sites of corresponding strain. We also computed the assay for transposase-accessible chromatin using sequencing (ATAC-seq) signal, which is independent of MACC, at different classes of boundaries in WT as well as in TKO strain. In both the strains, we observe that boundary strength scales with ATAC-seq score (Fig. 4E). Thus, accessibility, determined either by MACC or ATAC-seq, is a strong indicator of domain boundaries in vivo than occupancy. Nucleosome positioning dictates chromatin contacts As shown in Fig. 1B and reported before, TSS are the best genomic locations to compare nucleosome positioning, and many TSSs correspond to the domain boundaries (5). To study the relationship between nucleosome positioning and chromatin contacts, we compared the chromatin contacts from the WT and TKO strains around the TSS of the genes whose expression is not altered between WT and TKO strains (Fig. 5, A and B, and fig. S6), to avoid the possibility of any change in contacts arising due to transcription. RNA sequencing (RNA-seq) analysis shows that 367 genes are up-regulated and 62 are down-regulated in TKO strain in comparison to WT (fig. S7). The gene ontology analysis of differentially expressing genes does not show a pathway apparently linked to 3D chromatin organization (fig. S7F). MNase-seq data show that nucleosomes are well-positioned around the TSS in WT but not in the TKO condition at genes showing no significant change in expression level between the two strains (Fig. 5A). From the base-pair resolution composite plots of the contacts around the TSS of these genes, it is evident that chromatin topology in the TKO strain is different from that in the WT strain. We observed a fine pattern of contacts between different non-adjacent nucleosomes in case of the WT strain but no evidence of such a contact pattern in the case of the TKO strain (Fig. 5B and fig. S6). The pattern observed in WT seems to have got diffused out in TKO. From the differential plot (TKO/WT) of contact maps, we observe a moderate decrease in contacts between few nucleosomes close to TSS in TKO as compared to WT. There is moderate increase in contacts just upstream of the +1 nucleosome (corresponding to NDR) in TKO as compared to WT. As many of the NDR around TSS correspond to domain boundaries, this again shows that domain boundaries are weaker in TKO as compared to WT. Hence, these results show that nucleosome positioning strongly influences 3D chromatin organization, independent of transcription. To uncover the underlying molecular basis of decrease in shortrange contacts in TKO strain, we hypothesized that the change in the positioning of the nucleosomes along the chromatin could change the persistence length of the chromatin fiber along its contour, which, in turn, can make the chromatin fiber stiffer and decrease the probability of local chromatin contacts (Fig. 5, C and D) (26–28). To test whether the mere change in nucleosome positioning in the case of the TKO strain can influence the chromatin stiffness, we purified chromatin (devoid of nonhistone proteins) from both WT and TKO strains (Fig. 5F and fig. S8A), digested it with a restriction enzyme, and ligated it under diluted (10-fold) conditions favoring intramolecular circularization, followed by exonuclease treatment to digest linear DNA (Fig. 5E). Circularization requires end-to-end contact and thus provides a measure of contact probability. The circular DNA was quantified as the amount of ligated DNA detected after exonuclease treatment. We observed the

Hence, the change in chromatin contacts between WT and TKO does not arise due to a difference in transcription levels or due to a change in binding of Hho1 to the chromatin but due to the change in the nucleosome positioning. DISCUSSION

Here, we addressed the relationship between nucleosome distribution along the genome and higher-order organization of chromatin. We show that the change in the distribution of nucleosomes along the genome induced by loss of ISWI and CHD1 chromatin remodeling factors is associated with altered 3D chromatin organization at multiple scales. These results suggest that nucleosome landscape along the genome is an important determinant of higher-order 3D chromatin organization in vivo. Our in vitro experiments provide a possible biomechanical basis for observing change in chromatin contacts in the TKO strain. Our data show that perturbation of nucleosome positioning is accompanied by a decrease in short-range contacts. Well-positioned nucleosomes of the WT strain interact with each other establishing short-range contacts. In the case of the TKO strain, most of the nucleosomes are closely packed with a smaller fraction having longer spacing (29). In TKO strain, fewer short-range contacts are formed. A possible mechanistic explanation is that a nucleosome fiber with closely positioned nucleosomes will have a higher persistence length/ stiffness (as in TKO) than a nucleosome fiber with regularly positioned nucleosomes and intervening linkers (Fig. 5, C and D). Previous studies have also shown that more precise measurements of linker DNA persistence length or chromatin persistence length can better explain several features of 3D chromatin organization in yeast (31, 32). A polymer with a longer persistence length will be more difficult to bend than a polymer with a shorter persistence length (27, 33). Therefore, the probability of contact formation will be higher in flexible polymer with a smaller persistence length than in the stiffer polymer with a longer persistence length (Fig. 5C). Observation of more circular DNA in the WT chromatin as compared to TKO indicates more end-to-end contacts in WT and, thus, higher probability of chromatin contacts (Fig. 5, F and G). Therefore, we suggest that chromatin with proper linker lengths will be more flexible than chromatin with closely positioned nucleosomes, and thus, the chromatin of WT strain can form more contacts than that of TKO strain. This model rationalizes the pervasive and energetically costly activity of ATP-dependent nucleosome remodelers across the genome. Another possibility is that nucleosome spacing can influence chromatin compaction (17–19, 34), which, in turn, can affect the end-to-end ligation. Deletion of ISWI and CHD1 chromatin remodelers has also been reported to increase the cotranscriptional histone exchange at a subset of genes, resulting in incorporation of soluble acetylated histones into the chromatin (35). WT chromatin fragments with a relatively better nucleosome positioning can attain a compact conformation decreasing end-to-end spatial distance and favoring ligation. TKO chromatin fragments with more disturbed nucleosome positioning and elevated histone acetylation may not compact well, therefore decreasing the chances of ligation. Differences in chromatin compaction can result in differences in chromatin contacts observed by cross-linking–based methods like Micro-C XL. Our results

formation of more circularized DNA in the WT chromatin as compared to chromatin from the TKO strain (Fig. 5, F and G). The circularized DNA can arise from intramolecular ligation or from ligation of multiple DNA fragments. A ligation experiment performed at five times higher dilution (50-fold) yielded the similar result (fig. S8D), suggesting that most of the circular DNA arises from intramolecular ligation. To find whether nucleosome positioning of chromatin in vitro is different between two strains and similar to the in vivo positioning, we carried in vitro MNase-seq of chromatin purified from WT or TKO. We observed that in vitro nucleosomes are relatively well-positioned in WT but not in TKO (Fig. 5H), with some differences from in vivo MNase-seq (fig. S9). Broadening of nucleosomal peaks is observed in vitro as compared to in vivo (fig. S9, A and B), possibly due to some shifting of nucleosomes during chromatin purification or due to differential breathing of DNA wrapped around histone octamer in in vitro versus in situ condition (29). Under in vitro conditions, averaged nucleosome profile around TSS of genes shows that peaks corresponding to downstream nucleosomes (from +2) are relatively better resolved with respect to +1 nucleosome in WT but merged in TKO (fig. S9C). The extent of difference in nucleosome positioning between WT and TKO in vitro is enough to result only in twofold difference in ligation efficiency (Fig. 5G). Therefore, formation of more circular DNA in the case of WT chromatin indicates that the WT chromatin is possibly more flexible than TKO chromatin so that the ends can contact each other and get ligated more readily (Fig. 5D). According to Kratky and Porod model (26), end-to-end contact of a polymer depends on the contour length and the persistence length (Fig. 5, C and D). Therefore, probability of intramolecular ligation depends on the persistence length and contour length of chromatin fragments. Here, we digested the chromatin with DpnII and observed a similar size distribution of digested chromatin fragments (Fig. 5F), implying a similar contour length and leaving the persistence length as the only variable factor. Chromatin from the WT strain has regularly positioned nucleosomes with distinct linker regions, giving it more flexibility as compared to chromatin purified from TKO strain, which seems stiffer due to altered nucleosome positioning (Fig. 5, A, C, and D). We suggest that the flexibility arising due to more bending positions (linker regions) available in WT chromatin facilitates contacts between nonconsecutive nucleosomes (Fig. 5B and fig. S6). The change in nucleosome positioning in the TKO strain decreases the flexibility of chromatin, which, in turn, decreases the probability of nonconsecutive internucleosomal contacts as observed (Figs. 2, D and E, and 5B). There are clear changes in chromatin topology between WT and TKO, and this emphasizes that nucleosome positioning strongly influences 3D chromatin organization. Furthermore, ISWI and CHD1 chromatin remodelers have been reported to possibly influence the binding of histone H1 homolog, Hho1, to the chromatin (30). To find whether the binding of Hho1 to chromatin in vivo is altered in the TKO strain, which can influence chromatin contacts, we FLAG-tagged endogenous Hho1 in WT and TKO strains and carried cellular fractionation to determine the distribution of Hho1 in cytosolic, nuclear, and chromatin fractions (fig. S10, A and B). Quantitation of fraction of Hho1 in different cellular fractions shows that distribution of Hho1 among different cellular fractions is similar between the WT and TKO strains. This suggests that the fraction of Hho1 bound to the chromatin is similar in WT and TKO condition. We also determined the distribution of

In summary, we conclude that the hierarchical organization of chromatin has a “bottom-up” causation property by which the arrangement of nucleosomes at the primary level shapes the higher-order 3D organization, unraveling an important principle of 3D genome organization. Given the similarities in hierarchical chromatin organization between yeast and higher organisms as well as the conserved role of ISWI and CHD1 remodelers, we suggest that this bottom-up causation property of chromatin organization can also be conserved across species. MATERIALS AND METHODS

Yeast strains WT and TKO Saccharomyces cerevisiae strains (table S1) were provided by T. O. Hughes laboratory (Wellcome Trust Centre for Gene Regulation and Expression, College of Life Sciences, University of Dundee, DD15EH, UK). FLAGtagged Hho1- expressing strains were constructed by homologous recombination using 5× FLAG tag peptide and hph Mx6 as selectable marker (table S1). After transformation, yeast cells were selected on hygromycin yeast extract peptone dextrose (YPD) plates. MNase-titration-seq MNase-titration-seq was carried out as described previously (12, 23, 24) with some modifications. Yeast cells were grown in YPD media at 30°C up to optical density at 600 nm (OD600nm) of 2, cross-linked with 1% formaldehyde at room temperature (RT) (25°C) for 10 min, and quenched with 125 mM glycine (pH 7.5) at RT for 10 min. Cells were harvested at 5000 rpm for 5 min, washed three times with trisbuffered saline [20 mM tris (pH 7.5) and 120 mM NaCl] buffer, flash-frozen, and stored at −80°C. Cells were sensitized with sensitization buffer [100 mM tris HCl (pH 9.4) and 10 mM dithiothreitol (DTT)], and spheroplasts were prepared by using spheroplast buffer [1.2 M sorbitol and 20 mM Hepes (pH 7.5)] and addition of 100T Zymolyase (US Biologicals) at 30°C for 2 hours with shaking (210 rpm). Spheroplasts were pelleted down at 5000 rpm for 5 min and then washed once with spheroplast washing buffer [1.2 M sorbitol, 20 mM Hepes (pH 7.5), and 1 mM MgCl2] to stop Zymolyase digestion. Nuclei were isolated by incubation on ice for 30 min with 250 mM sucrose, 60 mM KCl, 1 mM phenylmethylsulfonyl fluoride (PMSF), 14 mM NaCl, 0.8% Triton X-100, 5 mM MgCl2 and 1 mM CaCl2 and then centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 5 min at 4°C. Nuclei pellet was washed three times with buffer A [10 mM tris HCl (pH 8), 0.5% NP-40, 75 mM NaCl, and 1 mM PMSF] and then three times with buffer B-400 [10 mM tris HCl (pH 8), 400 mM NaCl, and 1 mM PMSF] for different incubation time points on ice (15, 10, and 5 min). are in agreement with the recent in vitro study on a minimalistic system showing that nucleosome positioning set by ISWI and CHD1 chromatin remodelers is important for formation of chromatin domains (34). Altered 3D chromatin organization in TKO can also arise possibly due to the reason that nucleosome positioning is slightly different in different cells of the TKO strain, while in the WT strain, nucleosomes are well-positioned in all cells. As Micro-C XL is a population-based experiment, cell-to-cell variation in nucleosome positioning in TKO can result in variation in chromatin contacts and therefore leading to observation of diffused domains. On the other hand, well-positioned nucleosomes in WT cells lead to formation of distinct domains. In summary, all these possibilities suggest that nucleosome profile maintained by chromatin remodeling factors across the genome influences the 3D chromatin organization. Recently, several chromatin remodeling factors were screened for their role in chromatin organization (16). Depletion of CHD1 chromatin remodeling factor was shown to lead to decrease in shortrange contacts, while depletion of both ISW1 and CHD1 resulted in moderate decrease in short-range contacts. CHD1 and ISW1 have opposite effects on nucleosome spacing (30) and are involved in setting the nucleosome spacing at active genes, while ISW2 regulates the spacing at inactive genes (30). Therefore, the effects on nucleosome spacing are more severe in TKO than in double knockout strain. However, in this study (16), role of chromatin remodeling factors in formation of chromatin domains or boundaries was not studied. The decrease in short-range chromatin contacts seems to weaken the chromatin domains and decrease the strength of domain boundaries in the TKO strain. We observe that the width of NDR correlates with boundary strength. The nucleosome positioning is slightly different at boundaries in the TKO strain than that of the WT strain (Fig. 4, A and C). This can result in the weakening of boundaries separating the domains in the TKO strain. A recent in vitro study (34) also addressing the relationship between nucleosome positioning and domain formation reached to a similar conclusion that the width of NDR correlates with boundary strength, as shown by us in vivo. We observed that boundary strength strongly scales with accessibility. From both MACC and ATAC-seq, it is evident that the level of insulation at domain boundaries scales with their accessibility. Moreover, regions with higher MACC scores in WT than TKO have fewer contacts than corresponding regions from the TKO strain and vice versa. These observations suggest that the accessibility of chromatin at the nucleosome level is linked to the higher-order organization of chromatin, and ATAC/MACC score is a strong indicator of domain boundaries in vivo. Together, we have uncovered the role of ISWI and CHD1 chromatin remodeling factors in shaping the organization of chromatin from nucleosomes to chromosomes (Fig. 1A and fig. S10E). In a hierarchical organization like a pyramid of playing cards, lower levels can shape the higher levels (bottom-up causation), or higher levels can in turn influence lower levels (top-down causation) (24, 36). We tested the strength of the hierarchical nature of chromatin organization by perturbing the organization at the nucleosome level and monitored the effects on higher levels of organization and chromatin topology. High-throughput chromosome conformation capture In situ Hi-C was carried out according to the previously published protocol (37, 38) with some modifications. Yeast cells were grown up to OD600nm approximately 1, fixed with 3% formaldehyde for 20 min at RT, and quenched with 400 mM glycine (pH 7.5) for 6 min at RT with shaking. Cross-linked cells were harvested by centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 5 min, washed with sterile water, snap-frozen, and stored at −80°C. Cells were sensitized with sensitization buffer [100 mM tris HCl (pH 9.4) and 10 mM DTT] for 15 min at 30°C. Spheroplasts were prepared with 100 μl of Zymolyase (10 mg/ ml; US Biologicals) in spheroplast buffer [1.2 M sorbitol and 20 mM Hepes (pH 7.5)], incubated at 30°C for 90 min with shaking, and then washed once with spheroplast washing buffer [1.2 M sorbitol, 20 mM Hepes (pH 7.5) and 1 mM MgCl2] to stop Zymolyase digestion. Nuclei were isolated by incubating spheroplasts on ice for 30 min with nuclei isolation buffer (250 mM sucrose, 60 mM KCl, 1 mM PMSF, 14 mM NaCl, 0.8% Triton X-100, 5 mM MgCl2, and 1 mM CaCl2), dounced five times, and then centrifuged for 20 min at 14,000 rpm at 4°C. Nuclei pellet was resuspended in 360 μl of 1× NEBuffer 3.1, and 0.1% SDS was added, incubated at 65°C for 10 min, and then quenched by 1% Triton X-100. Chromatin was digested with 400 U of DpnII [New England Biolabs (NEB)] by incubation at 37°C overnight. The next day, 100 U of DpnII was added, again incubated for 2 hours at 37°C, and then heat-inactivated at 65°C for 20 min. The digested overhangs were filled and marked by biotin with 60 μl of fill-in reaction mixture containing 250 μM each of biotin-dATP, deoxycytidine triphosphate (dCTP), deoxythymidine triphosphate (dTTP), deoxyguanosine triphosphate (dGTP) (Invitrogen) and 40 U of Klenow large fragment (NEB) and incubated for 4 hours at 23°C. Biotinylated DNA fragments were ligated with 700 μl of ligation reaction mixture containing 1× ligase buffer, 240 U of T4 DNA ligase (Thermo Fisher Scientific), 1× bovine serum albumin (BSA), and 1% Triton X-100, incubated for 4 hours at 16°C with slow rotation, and centrifuged at 14,000 rpm for 20 min at 4°C, and then the pellet was resuspended in 1× NEBuffer 2. RNase A treatment was done at 37°C for 30 min, followed by proteinase K treatment at 55°C for 1 hour and then incubation at 65°C for overnight to reverse cross-links. DNA was extracted with phenol:chloroform and then precipitated with absolute ethanol. Biotin was removed from free ends by adding 9 U of T4 DNA polymerase (NEB), 25 μM each

of dATP and dGTP, incubated for 4 hours at 20°C in a thermal cycler and then for 20 min at 75°C to inactivate the enzyme. DNA samples were washed using 500 μl of 30-kDa Amicon filters and then sheared to a size of 200 to 700 bp by sonication using the program: 10 s ON, 59 s OFF, amplitude 20%, 12 times. Size selection in the range of 300 to 500 was done using Ampure XP beads (Beckman Coulter), and then biotinylated junctions were pulled down by Dynabeads MyOne Streptavidin C1 beads (Invitrogen) which were first blocked with salmon sperm DNA solution (0.2 mg/ml) for 30 min at RT with rotation and then washed twice with 1× Tween Wash Buffer (TWB) [5 mM tris-HCl (pH 7.5), 0.5 mM EDTA, 1 M NaCl, and 0.05% Tween 20]. Beads were resuspended in 300 μl of 2× binding buffer [10 mM tris-HCl (pH 7.5), 1 mM EDTA, and 2 M NaCl], added to the equal volume of DNA sample, and incubated at RT for 90 min with rotation. Beads were separated, washed twice with 1× TWB, and used for the preparation of the Hi-C sequencing library. First, end repairing was done by 100 μl of reaction mixture [1× ligase buffer, 0.5 mM deoxynucleotide triphosphates (dNTP) mix, 50 U of T4 polynucleotide kinase (NEB), 12 U of T4 DNA polymerase I (NEB), and 5 U of Large Klenow Fragment (NEB)], incubated at 20°C for 30 min, followed by A-tailing in 100 μl of reaction mixture [1× NEBuffer 2, 0.5 mM dATP, and 25 U Klenow exo-minus (NEB)] and incubation at 37°C for 30 min. Then, adapters were ligated with 50 μl of reaction mixture [1× Quick ligase buffer, 2 μl of annealed adapters, and 2 μl of Quick ligase (NEB)] and incubated at RT for 15 min. After washing twice with 1× TWB, beads were resuspended in 1× tris (pH 8), and the Hi-C library was directly amplified from beads using a reaction mixture containing Pfu Ultra II fusion HS polymerase (Agilent), universal primer, indexed primer, and program: 98°C (30 s); 11 cycles of 98°C (10 s), 65°C (30 s), and 72°C (45 s); and 72°C (5 min) and hold at 4°C. The polymerase chain reaction (PCR)–amplified Hi-C library was purified by 1× Ampure XP beads (Beckman Coulter), and the efficiency of the Hi-C library was checked by digestion with ClaI (NEB) and DpnII (NEB) at 37°C overnight. The final Hi-C libraries were paired-end (2 × 150) sequenced using the Illumina sequencing HiSeq 2500 platform.

Analysis of Hi-C and Micro-C XL data Fastq files were processed with distiller (41) to generate pairs and mcool files. In more detail, reads were mapped against the sacCer3 reference genome (table S2). The mcool files were generated with 0.1-, 0.2-, 0.5-, 1-, 2.5-, 5-, 10-, 25-, 50-, 100-kbp bin sizes. To address potential biases caused by dinucleosomal contaminants in Micro-C XL reads (distances < 500 bp), the mcool files were balanced by ignoring the first five diagonals during ICE (iterative correction and eigenvector decomposition) normalization. HiCRep was used to confirm the reproducibility, and merged data were used for further analysis. Centromere analysis The positions of centromeres were obtained from (42). Each combination of centromere-centromere interactions was exported with cooltools from respective mcool files in Jupiter-Notebooks to text files (±50 kbp, bin size = 1 kbp). The average interactions within a ±10-kbp region around the central pixel were divided by the mean interactions across all four corners in both Hi-C and Micro-C XL matrices (fig. S4A). The mean interactions of all centromere-centromere matrices were then calculated, and the difference between TKO and WT experiments for each pixel was computed. The resulting matrices were visualized in R (fig. S4). Interaction decay curves P(s) The interaction decay curves were computed for paired files with duplicates removed “*.no.dups.valid.pairs” output from distiller (41) First, reads mapping trans-chromosomal interactions were excluded from the analysis. The read positions were shifted by 73 bp to theoretical nucleosome dyads positions according to read orientations. The mean frequency of genomic distances between read pairs was computed for exponentially increasing bins and divided by the total number of reads to obtain the read density as a function of distance. Similarly, the interaction decay curves were computed per chromosome, and the differential for WT and TKO was computed and plotted in R (Fig. 2E). Insulation scores The boundary strength and the insulation score were computed with the cooltools (43) insulation function using 100-bp sliding window and a 5-kbp sliding diamond (--ignore-diags 5). The boundary score was used for thresholding as indicated in Fig. 3 (C to E). For stratification into quantiles, equally sized list for all boundaries with a boundary score higher than 0.1 were considered (Fig. 3). On diagonal 3D pile-ups We used cooltools (43) to generate contact frequency pileup maps centered on specific genomic locations using public jupyter notebooks (https://github.com/hms-dbmi/hic-data-analysis-bootcamp/ blob/master/notebooks/06_analysis_cooltools-snipping-pileups. ipynb) (accessed on 20 June 2022). Here, we used a resolution of 0.1 kb with a 5-kb window on each side of the loop. The color range for plotting was chosen to span 5 to 95% of the value range in WT and TKO experiments for any given condition in log10 space. The differential pileups were generated by dividing observedoverexpected log space matrices. Base-pair resolution plots The base-pair resolution plots were calculated in R from pairs files (distiller output) (41). First, the start positions for reads 1 and 2 were shifted by 73 bp in direction of read orientations to obtain theoretical nucleosome dyads. Next, reads with negative positions, distance between read 1 and read 2 smaller than 100 bp, and reads with forward-reverse orientations were excluded from further analysis.

RNA sequencing RNA extraction from yeast cells was done according to the previously described protocol (45) with some modifications. Yeast cells were grown in YPD media up to OD600nm approximately 1, harvested by centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 5 min at 4°C, washed twice with cold DEPC water, snap-frozen, and stored at −80°C. Cells were resuspended in 600 μl of TES buffer [10 mM tris HCl (pH 7.5), 10 mM EDTA, and 0.5% SDS], and then 600 μl of acidic phenol:chloroform was added and incubated at 65°C for 1 hour with interval mixing. Centrifugation was done for 10 min at 13,000 rpm at RT. RNA was purified using acidic phenol:chloroform and precipitated by absolute ethanol. Pellet was resuspended in RNase-free water, and deoxyribonuclease treatment was done by incubating at 37°C for 10 min, stopped by the addition of 5 mM EDTA, and then heat-inactivated at 75°C for 10 min. RNA samples were then purified using an RNeasy kit (Qiagen), and the quality of the RNA samples was checked by a Qubit RNA IQ assay kit. From the total RNA, ribosomal RNA was depleted by using the RiboZero gold magnetic kit, and then cDNA was generated with the TruSeq nonstranded kit. Sequencing libraries for next-generation sequencing were prepared (using the Illumina Kit) and sequenced using the Illumina sequencing HiSeq 2500 platform. RNA-seq analysis was done as previously described (23). Briefly, TopHat software was used to align RNA-seq tags using default parameters, and HTseq was used to count the number of the tags aligned to coding regions. After RNA-seq, tag frequencies had been normalized for GC content using bioconductor package EDASeq, and the expression estimates for each gene and differential expression were then calculated using the bioconductor software DESeq. The gene ontology and Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) analyses were performed in R statistical environment with GO.db and KEGG.db Bioconductor packages, respectively. The supernatant was decanted, and the cell pellets were then washed in 30 ml of spheroplast buffer [1.2 M sorbitol and 20 mM Hepes (pH 7.5)] and centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 5 min at 4°C. Harvested cell pellets were resuspended in 40 ml of spheroplast buffer. Zymolyase digestion was carried out at 30°C and 210 rpm, till OD600 dropped to 1/10th of initial OD600. Reaction was stopped by centrifuging cells at 5000 rpm for 5 min at 4°C. The pellets were then resuspended in 40 ml of spheroplast wash buffer [1.2 M sorbitol, 20 mM Hepes (pH 7.5), and 1 mM MgCl2] and centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 5 min at 4°C. Nuclei were then extracted from spheroplasts by sequentially resuspending and incubating pellet in 25 ml of nuclei isolation buffer (250 mM sucrose, 60 mM KCl, 1 mM PMSF, 14 mM NaCl, 0.8% Triton X-100, 5 mM MgCl2, and 1 mM CaCl2), three times with incubation times of 20, 10, and 5 min, respectively, on ice. Each incubation was followed by centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 5 min at 4°C. The nuclear pellet was then washed with 25 ml of buffer A [10 mM tris HCl (pH 8), 0.5% NP-40, 75 mM NaCl, and 1 mM PMSF] three times with incubation times of 15, 10, and 5 min on ice, respectively, and each time centrifugation was done at 5000 rpm for 5 min at 4°C. Again, the nuclear pellet was washed with buffer B-400 [10 mM tris HCl (pH 8), 400 mM NaCl, and 1 mM PMSF] and centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 5 min at 4°C, three times, with incubation periods of 10, 5, and 5 min, respectively, on ice. Nuclear isolation was then followed by resuspension of nuclei in 40 ml of medium salt buffer [20 mM Hepes (pH 8), 400 mM NaCl, 1 mM EDTA, 5% glycerol, 1 mM PMSF, 1 mM β- mercaptoethanol, and 1× protease inhibitor cocktail] and transfer into a 40-ml Dounce homogenizer. Thirty gentle strokes were given by pestle B, followed by 20-min incubation on ice and centrifuged at 11,000 rpm for 20 min at 4°C. The supernatant was discarded, and the pellets were then resuspended in 6 ml of high-salt buffer [20 mM Hepes (pH 8), 650 mM NaCl, 1 mM EDTA, 340 mM sucrose, 5% glycerol, 1 mM PMSF, 1 mM β-mercaptoethanol, and 1× protease inhibitor cocktail] and transferred to a 7-ml Dounce homogenizer. Four hundred strokes of pestle B were given, followed by centrifugation at 11,000 rpm for 25 min at 4°C. The supernatant was collected and dialyzed against low-salt buffer [20 mM Hepes (pH 8), 100 mM NaCl, 1 mM EDTA,1 mM PMSF, and 1 mM β-mercaptoethanol] for 2 hours at 4°C on a magnetic stirrer. After dialysis, samples were centrifuged at 11,000 rpm for 20 min at 4°C to get rid of any insoluble fraction. The supernatant was then concentrated, using a 30-kDa cutoff concentrator, to a volume of ~800 μl. Forty micrograms of both WT and TKO was run on 15% SDS-PAGE and stained by Coomassie brilliant blue dye.
